title: Angela predicts she will be fired — Kevin agrees sincerely | self-martyrdom
given_circumstances: Downsizing anxiety has reached the accounting department. Angela is processing threat through her characteristic framework of resigned righteousness.
full_dialogue:
Angela: I bet it's gonna be me. Probably gonna be me.
Kevin: Yeah, it'll be you.
scene_arc: Angela predicts her own firing as a performance of dignified resignation. Kevin agrees with complete sincerity, not realizing this is not the response she wanted.
motivation: To voice her anxiety in a way that invites reassurance. Angela wants someone to say 'no, of course not you' — she is fishing, not predicting.
internal_thoughts: If anyone is going to be let go it will be me because that's how things work for people who do everything right. The universe is not fair. Kevin will tell me I'm wrong. [Kevin agrees] He agreed. Kevin agreed with me.
reflection: Kevin has no filter but he also has no malice. He simply said what he thought. That is somehow worse than if he meant it. I need to be more careful who I process fear with.

title: Accounting department self-introductions — Angela describes Oscar as a stapler she has to push | control
given_circumstances: The accounting team gives their introductions to the documentary. Angela goes first and frames her colleagues as burdens she manages.
full_dialogue:
Angela: My name is Angela and um, I'm in charge of the accounting department. There are three of us, Kevin and Oscar.
Oscar: It's my job to keep the books in order. And, um, I... I'm, uh, immediately below my supervisor.
Angela: Oscar, how do I describe him. He's like a stapler. Do I need a stapler? Yes. But, I'm still the one that has to push it down.
Oscar: There's a lot of anxiety. I don't want to get laid off. This place is like five minutes from my house.
Kevin: I don't wanna be laid off. This is a good job for me. 'Cause I need my nights free. I'm in a band.
Angela: Not very worried about downsizing right now. Because, uh, I think you've met Kevin and um...
Oscar: Can I just say one thing? Um, my friend is Jewish, Art Geller. And a couple of years ago he, um, showed me how you can plant a tree in Israel, so I planted a tree. So... You know 'cause... Yeah we work in paper. Well, you know, I try to give back.
Angela: I have a lot of cat figurines on my desk. I think cats, really, are old souls. And, uh, Phyllis thinks so too.
scene_arc: Three separate self-portraits: Angela as the indispensable authority, Oscar as the anxious pragmatist, Kevin as the guileless honest one. A complete accounting tableau.
motivation: To establish herself as the load-bearing member of the accounting department and subtly position Kevin as the redundancy.
internal_thoughts: I am in charge of this department. Oscar is functional but requires management. Kevin... I implied it without saying it. Not worried about downsizing because of Kevin and um. They'll understand. My cats are a comfort. Old souls.
reflection: I am the one who holds this together. That is true. The stapler metaphor was good — Oscar is useful but I am the force that makes him useful. This is an accurate description.

title: Michael coaches Kevin and Angela to go further in the exercise | pushing
given_circumstances: The race-card exercise is ongoing. Kevin and Angela are interacting and Michael is watching, pushing them to go further.
full_dialogue:
Kevin: Hey.
Angela: Hey.
Kevin: You wanna go to the beach?
Angela: Sure.
Kevin: You wanna get high?
Angela: No.
Kevin: I think you do, mon.
Angela: Stop...
Michael: OK. All right. No. It's good. You just need to push it. You need to go a little bit further. All right. OK.
scene_arc: Michael coaches Kevin and Angela through an exchange during the race card exercise, pushing them to go further. They're doing their best. Michael wants more.
motivation: To play along at the absolute minimum level and end the interaction as fast as possible.
internal_thoughts: Kevin asked if I want to get high. No. He did the accent. I told him to stop. Michael says we need to push further. I am not pushing further. I am standing here until this ends.
reflection: I participated. That's all anyone can ask. I said 'no' and 'stop' and Michael still said it was good. It wasn't good. Nothing about this is good.

title: Oscar, Kevin, and Angela react to Michael's refusal to leave his office | helplessness
given_circumstances: Oscar, Kevin, and Angela are gathered together, quietly fuming about the health care plan and Michael's refusal to emerge from his office.
full_dialogue:
Oscar: He literally won't come out of his office.
Kevin: He's got to come out sometime. To go to the bathroom.
Angela: Kevin! That's inappropriate.
scene_arc: Oscar, Kevin, and Angela react to Michael's refusal to emerge. Kevin notes the bathroom problem. Angela shuts him down. The group is unified in frustration and helpless.
motivation: To maintain standards of conversation even in a crisis.
internal_thoughts: Kevin mentioned the bathroom. That is not a workplace conversation. I said his name and the word inappropriate. That should be sufficient.
reflection: I corrected Kevin. The group continues.

title: Oscar and Angela debrief — Angela calls Oscar out for letting Michael escape | accountability
given_circumstances: Oscar, Kevin, and Angela are together after the confrontation with Michael. They are processing what just happened.
full_dialogue:
Oscar: This is not good.
Angela: It's ridiculous. Did you talk to him?
Oscar: What was that?
Angela: You let him walk all over you. It's just pathetic.
Kevin: What are you guys talking about?
Angela: Nothing, Kevin.
scene_arc: Oscar and Angela debrief after Michael's non-response. Angela accuses Oscar of being walked over. Kevin asks what they're talking about. Angela shuts him out. The coalition is fractured by internal contempt.
motivation: To hold the coalition to a higher standard of resistance.
internal_thoughts: Oscar let Michael off the hook with the surprise announcement. I said so directly. He deflects with a question. I called it pathetic. Kevin asked what we're talking about. I said nothing, Kevin. That's the correct answer.
reflection: I said the hard thing. Oscar received it poorly. Kevin is not part of this conversation.

title: Michael performs a drum roll with no surprise inside it — and stays in the drum roll | self-deception
characters_involved: Angela, Dwight, Michael, Oscar, Pam, Stanley
given_circumstances: The end of day has arrived. The staff has assembled outside Michael's office expecting the promised surprise. Michael has no surprise and must now face them.
full_dialogue:
Oscar: Well?
Michael: Well, what? You could be referring to anything.
Oscar: OK, the health care plan.
Pam: Why did you put Dwight in charge of that? He did a horrible job.
Michael: Uh, Dwight? Did you raise benefits?
Dwight: I most certainly did not.
Michael: Oh come on! That's horrible! Aaah... Thanks, Dwight, for a crappy plan. Ah, Damn! Oh, mmm, I wish I had time to change it, but Jan needs it by five, and... what time is it, what time is it? [looks at watch] Ah, it's after five. Oh, oh it's awful. So, well, OK. See you guys on Monday.
Angela: What about the surprise?
Michael: Oh... Yes. Exactly. Thank you Angela, for reminding me. Terrific. Um, before I tell everybody what the big surprise is, would you like to tell me what you think the big surprise is?
Stanley: We all think you don't have a surprise.
Michael: All right, I have some news for you. There is a big surprise. And... here it is. Here we go. And the big surprise is... Brrrrrrrr! Drum roll... Brrrrrrrr! Brrrrrrrr!
scene_arc: The office assembles for the promised surprise. Michael throws Dwight under the bus for the plan, uses the clock to dodge fixing it, and then — when pushed about the surprise — performs a drum roll that goes nowhere.
motivation: To hold Michael to the promise he made.
internal_thoughts: He was going to leave without addressing the surprise. I asked. I had to ask. He pivoted to drum rolls. Asking was the right move. Whatever comes next is better than him walking out.
reflection: I forced the moment. The drum roll is what I got. It's something.

title: The party planning committee debates streamer colors — Angela rules green whoreish | control
given_circumstances: The party planning committee — Angela, Pam, and Phyllis — is meeting. Angela is in charge. The only item on the agenda so far is streamers.
full_dialogue:
Phyllis: Well, uh, for decorations, maybe we could... it's stupid, forget it.
Angela: What?
Phyllis: I was just going to say, maybe we could have streamers, but that's dumb, everybody has streamers. Never mind.
Angela: No, yeah, I think that's a good idea.
Phyllis: Yeah?
Angela: What color do you guys think?
Phyllis: Well, there's green, um, blue... yellow... red...
Pam: How about green?
Angela: I think green is kind of whoreish.
scene_arc: The party planning committee debates streamers. Phyllis suggests green. Angela declares green whoreish. The committee is not functioning.
motivation: To maintain standards. Green is not an appropriate color for a workplace party.
internal_thoughts: Phyllis suggested streamers. I supported it. Then Pam said green. Green is whoreish. I said so. I will not apologize for having standards.
reflection: Green was eliminated. The committee will find a better option.

title: Michael pushes ice-cream cake despite Meredith's dairy allergy — it's not just about her | self-centeredness
given_circumstances: Michael has convened with the party planning committee to weigh in on the cake. Angela has information Michael doesn't want.
full_dialogue:
Michael: These are my party-planning beeyatches. Pulled off an amazing '80s party last year. Off the hook!
Michael: So I was thinking, if you haven't already got a cake, um, maybe going for one of those ice-cream cakes from Baskin-Robbins. Those are very good. Very Delicious.
Angela: Meredith's allergic to dairy, so...
Michael: She's not the only one that's going to be eating it, right? I think everybody likes ice-cream cake. It's not, uh, it's not just about her, so...
Pam: It is... her birthday.
Michael: Mint chocolate chip! That'd be good, how about some, mint chocolate chip?
scene_arc: Michael joins the party planning committee to push for an ice-cream cake. Angela informs him Meredith is dairy-allergic. Michael deflects — other people will eat it. Pam notes it is Meredith's birthday. Michael pivots to mint chocolate chip.
motivation: To ensure the party respects Meredith's dietary restrictions and that the committee's decisions are sound.
internal_thoughts: Meredith is allergic to dairy. I said so. Michael dismissed it. Pam supported the point. Michael pivoted to a different flavor of the same dairy cake. The information was given. He chose not to use it. That is his choice and his consequence.
reflection: I provided the relevant information. Michael overrode it. The committee's integrity is intact. The outcome is his responsibility.

title: The surprise party begins — Happy Birthday sung tunelessly | performance
given_circumstances: The surprise birthday party is beginning. The office has gathered. Michael is leading the Happy Birthday.
full_dialogue:
Everybody: Surprise!
Meredith: Oh! Surprise.
Angela: No, it's ah...
Michael: It's surprise Meredith. One, two...
Everybody: [tunelessly] Happy birthday to you.
Michael: Find a key.
Everybody: Happy birthday...
scene_arc: The surprise party begins. Everyone sings. Michael tries to find the right key.
motivation: To correct the surprise framing — this is a birthday party, not just a surprise.
internal_thoughts: She said 'surprise' and I started to clarify the occasion. Michael talked over it.
reflection: Michael talked over my clarification. The party continues.

title: Meredith reads birthday cards aloud — Michael's escalates to hysterectomy joke — the room splinters | harm
characters_involved: Angela, Everybody, Meredith, Michael, Oscar, Pam, Phyllis
given_circumstances: The party is at its peak. Meredith is reading birthday cards aloud at Michael's request. Michael's card is incoming.
full_dialogue:
Pam: Happy Birthday. [gives Meredith her card]
Michael: Read it out loud. And say who wrote everything so we know whose is the best.
Meredith: 'Happy Bird-day' Um... 'Meredith, good news. You're not actually a year older because you work here, where time stands still.'
Michael: [under his breath] I don't know about that.
Meredith: That was Stanley. 'Meredith, happy birthday, you're the best. Love, Pam.'
Michael: [pretends to vomit] Huh! Thanks, downer.
Meredith: This is from Michael. 'Meredith, let's hope the only downsizing that happens to you is that someone downsizes your age.'
Michael: Because of the downsizing. Rumors. And because you're gettin' old.
Meredith: No, I... I get it. It's funny.
Michael: [laughs] You didn't get the joke. So, that's cool. That's, you know what? Actually... I have a bunch of these, good ones, that I didn't use. Um... Oh, where's that? Oh, OK, here's a good one. Um... 'Hey Meredith, Liz Taylor called, she wants her age back and her divorces back.' 'Cause Meredith's been divorced like, twice. Is that right?
Meredith: You're right. You're right. Yes.
Michael: Divorce. Um... OK, 'Meredith is so old...'
Oscar: How old is she?
Michael: Everybody? If... could do it? 'Meredith is so old...'
Everybody: How old is she?
Michael: She's so old, she went into an antique store and they kept her.'
Michael: That wasn't even mine. I got that off the Internet. Website. Um, don't get mad at me.
Oscar: Uh, nice party Michael.
Michael: This isn't my fault. Ladies, not your best effort. The streamers? I think we could have done better than that.
Angela: Phyllis wanted red, I didn't.
Phyllis: Oh, boy... You...
Michael: OK, we... all right. People, hold on, hold on. Just a second. OK, I think we're losing sight of what is really important here. And that is that we are... a group of people... who work together. I was... I really wasn't gonna flaunt this. I have made a very sizable donation to Oscar's nephew's... walkathon. $25.
Oscar: Per mile.
Michael: Per mile, yes.
scene_arc: Meredith reads the birthday cards aloud. Michael's card lands badly. He escalates into backup material including Liz Taylor, the antique store joke, and finally the hysterectomy/Michael Jackson comparison. The room splinters. Michael pivots to the walkathon donation as redemption.
motivation: To defend her streamer decision when Michael attacks the decoration choices.
internal_thoughts: He said the streamers were not our best effort. Phyllis wanted red. I didn't. I said so on the record. Michael moved on before either of us could finish.
reflection: I clarified the streamer decision responsibility. The record is corrected.

title: Oscar presents the walkathon sheet to Angela — she donates but requires no mailing list | transactional
given_circumstances: Oscar is circulating the walkathon sponsorship sheet. Angela is at her desk.
full_dialogue:
Angela: What is this?
Oscar: My nephew does it every year. Anything you could give would be fantastic.
Angela: Okay, but I don't want to be put on a mailing list.
scene_arc: Oscar presents the walkathon sponsorship sheet to Angela. She agrees to donate but requires a no-mailing-list guarantee.
motivation: To donate to the walkathon while protecting herself from future solicitations.
internal_thoughts: Oscar wants a walkathon donation. I'm willing to give. I need assurance about the mailing list. That is a reasonable condition.
reflection: The no-mailing-list condition was stated. Oscar will honor it or he won't. I've done my part.

title: Angela discovers Dwight has the first-aid kit and reclaims it | control
given_circumstances: Angela is looking for the first-aid kit before the game. Dwight has it.
full_dialogue: Angela: Has anyone seen the first-aid kit? [Dwight holds the kit up] How many times have I told you? I'm the safety officer, not you.
scene_arc: Angela discovers Dwight has the first-aid kit. She reclaims it by citing her role as safety officer.
motivation: To reclaim the first-aid kit and reassert her safety officer authority.
internal_thoughts: Dwight has the kit. I'm the safety officer. Not him. How many times. I said it. He should know.
reflection: Kit reclaimed. The boundary was stated.

title: Michael gets elbowed, calls the game, claims the win, tries to collect the Saturday bet, and gives everyone the day off | authority
characters_involved: Angela, Darryl, Dwight, Lonny, Michael, Roy
given_circumstances: The game is in its final stretch. Michael gets elbowed, calls the game, declares his team the winner by default, tries to enforce the Saturday work bet, then backs down and cancels it.
full_dialogue:
Michael: [Phyllis scores] Yeah! Yeah! Yeah! In, your, face! Angela, what's the score?
Angela: You're ahead.
Michael: Yeah, baby, here we go!
Michael: [Jim has the ball] Jim! Right here! [runs into the elbow of the guy guarding him] Ow! God! Hold it!
Michael: Foul! Foul!
Michael: That was a flagrant, personal, intentional foul. Right there.
Michael: [mocking voice] Yes, it was. You know what, I'm just being fair.
Michael: Game over. Game over. That is it! I'm sorry, you know? I hate to do it this way but, you know, that's just... we're having a friendly game. It's a shame. This is a damn shame, but we're like a family here and that just, that won't fly.
Dwight: Here, give me that. You have to break the interior bag. [bag explodes]
Michael: Thanks Dwight.
Lonny: Wait, what does that mean? What is it, a tie? What's going on?
Michael: Well, let's just say whoever was ahead won.
Darryl: That was you.
Michael: It was us? Really? I didn't, I didn't know. Great, I mean, I guess you guys are working Saturday. Your face.
Roy: No, no, no, I'm not coming in on Saturday.
Darryl: Yeah, this isn't happening.
Michael: Um... well, you guys, you know, I'm the boss so...
Lonny: So what's that? We're coming in on Monday, right?
Michael: [laughing] You guys believed me? Come on. Dogs, you know, you should know me better than that. No, oh, do you think that would've been good for morale? No. No. No. Exactly, no. I'm embarrassed it was even that close though. So... nah, of course, we're coming in Saturday. Good game. Word.
scene_arc: Michael gets elbowed, calls the game over, declares his team winner by default, attempts to collect the Saturday bet, then backs down when challenged, cancels the Saturday requirement entirely, and exits with 'Word.'
motivation: To keep score accurately and report it when asked.
internal_thoughts: He asked the score. We're ahead. I said so. That's my role.
reflection: Score reported accurately.

title: Dwight eats Angela's candy and uses scheduling power to keep her from enforcing the one-per-person rule | power
given_circumstances: Dwight is eating Angela's desk candy. Angela invokes the one-per-person rule. Dwight uses his scheduling power as leverage.
full_dialogue:
Dwight: [eating Tootsie Rolls from Angela's candy on her desk] Mmm. Good. Mmm.
Angela: They're one per person. [points to sign 'Please take one!!']
Dwight: Would you like to have a vacation this year? That's what I thought. [continues eating Tootsie Rolls] Mmm, delicious.
scene_arc: Dwight eats multiple pieces of Angela's desk candy. Angela cites the one-per-person rule. Dwight leverages the Saturday schedule. Angela backs down.
motivation: To enforce the one-per-person rule and protect her candy.
internal_thoughts: He's taking multiple Tootsie Rolls. The sign says one per person. I said so. He threatened my vacation. I backed down. He kept eating.
reflection: I backed down. The candy is gone. The scheduling power is real.

title: Dwight is wearing a face guard; his CPR credentials are county-certified but Michael's face wasn't for it | exclusion
given_circumstances: Dwight is wearing a face guard near the sidelines. Angela is objecting to him holding the first-aid kit.
full_dialogue:
Angela: [Dwight holds the first aid kit up] I'm the safety officer, not you.
Dwight: Isn't that crazy? I'm a Volunteer Sheriff's Deputy. Lackawanna County says that it's okay for me to perform CPR, but for Michael my lips aren't qualified enough for his perfect little face. It's nuts.
Angela: Is that really necessary?
Dwight: [wearing a face guard] I've almost had my nose broken a dozen times.
scene_arc: Dwight is wearing a face guard and holding the first-aid kit. Angela reclaims the kit. Dwight explains that his CPR certification is valid in the county but Michael excluded him from the game. He has almost had his nose broken twelve times.
motivation: To reclaim the first-aid kit and question the face guard.
internal_thoughts: He has the kit again. I'm the safety officer. I said so. He explained the CPR situation. I asked if the face guard is really necessary. He said twelve times.
reflection: Kit retrieved. The face guard is apparently necessary. Twelve times is a lot.

title: Dwight attempts to ask Katy out in private then publicly; she says no to both; Angela chooses gray | rejection
given_circumstances: Katy is working with Angela in the conference room. Dwight arrives and attempts to ask Katy out privately.
full_dialogue:
Katy: You seem to like to touch things. Did you try the velvet?
Angela: I don't like to necessarily touch things. I'm just... I'm shopping.
Katy: Oh no, it's fine that you, um. Here, what about the raspberry one? It's really uh, kind of festive. It's got a lot of personality.
Angela: Yeah, uh no.
Dwight: Hey, how's it going? Good. Can I talk to you for a second? In private?
Katy: I don't think so I'm really busy.
Dwight: It will just take a second.
Katy: I can't.
Dwight: Just for a minute.
Katy: I really can't.
Dwight: Please? I wanted to talk to you in private because I wanted to ask you out on a date.
Katy: No.
Dwight: Ok was that no to talking to me in private, or was that no to the date?
Katy: Both.
Katy: What colors do you like?
Angela: Gray. Dark Gray. Charcoal.
scene_arc: Angela is shopping coldly. Dwight arrives and attempts a private ask. Katy refuses twice and redirects. Dwight escalates to the public ask. Katy says no to both. Angela chooses gray.
motivation: To find a purse that is acceptable to her standards.
internal_thoughts: She touched the velvet. I was shopping, not touching. The raspberry is festive — no. Dwight interrupted. He got rejected. I chose gray. Dark gray. Charcoal.
reflection: Rejected festive. Chose gray. Acceptable outcome.

title: Michael presents Tight Ass to Angela citing her caboose; she says no | rejection
given_circumstances: Michael is presenting the Tight Ass award to Angela.
full_dialogue:
Michael: And the 'Tight Ass' award goes to Angela. Not only because she is everybody's favorite stickler, but because she has, a great caboose. So...come on down.
Angela: No.
scene_arc: Michael presents the Tight Ass award to Angela, citing both her stickler reputation and her caboose. Angela says no.
motivation: To decline the Tight Ass award.
internal_thoughts: No.
reflection: No.

title: Angela is the designated driver and no one covers her gas | consequence
given_circumstances: Angela is speaking to the documentary camera about being the designated driver.
full_dialogue: Angela: Yes, I'm the designated driver, which is very important. We need everyone to get home safely. [sighs] But no one ever gives me gas money, and people live all over. It's expensive.
scene_arc: Angela explains her designated driver situation and notes no one covers her gas.
motivation: To explain her designated driver role and register the financial imposition.
internal_thoughts: I'm the designated driver. Very important. Everyone gets home safely. No one gives me gas money. People live all over. It's expensive.
reflection: Designated driver. No gas money. The service is provided. The expense is real.

title: Dwight asks Angela about the bathroom wall content; offers a butt-or-wiener nod system | absurdism
given_circumstances: Dwight is attempting to get Angela to tell him what was written on the bathroom wall about Michael.
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Angela, it's simple. Just tell me what the bathroom wall says.
Angela: I don't talk about what goes on in the restroom.
Dwight: I'm not asking what you do in there. I just want to know what's written about Michael.
Angela: I don't says those kinds of things out loud. It's inappropriate.
Dwight: Okay, I'll talk. You nod if I get it right. Does it have to do with his butt or his wiener?
scene_arc: Dwight asks Angela about the bathroom wall content. She refuses on grounds of propriety. Dwight guesses: butt or wiener.
motivation: To refuse to engage with bathroom wall content under any circumstances.
internal_thoughts: I don't talk about what goes on in the restroom. I don't say those kinds of things out loud. It's inappropriate. Butt or wiener. He said that. I did not nod.
reflection: I did not nod. The content of bathrooms is private. I will not participate in this regardless of the framing.

title: Michael hijacks the end of the seminar; Stanley's daughter photo comes down; Pam's request is made | harm
characters_involved: Angela, Dwight, Kevin, Meredith, Michael, Pam, Phyllis, Stanley, Toby
given_circumstances: The harassment seminar is ending. Pam asks the office to behave in front of her mother. Michael arrives late and hijacks the conclusion.
full_dialogue:
Pam: She's coming in today and maybe just don't joke around about that stuff in front of her.
Toby: Great point.
Pam: Thank you.
Toby: Um... in fact, basic rule of thumb, let's just act everyday like Pam's Mom's coming in. All right. That's it. Um... if anybody has any questions about anything, you know where I sit in the back.
Michael: Hi, is it over?
Toby: Uh, yes!
Michael: No.
Toby: I can go over it with you.
Michael: I know, I know. It's good. It is not over. It is not over til it's over.
Toby: It's over.
Michael: Did he tell you everything? Obviously, he didn't because you all still look relatively happy. Albeit bored. Do you realize what we're losing? Seriously?
Angela: Email forwards.
Michael: Exactly! Mmwwah [blows kiss to Angela]! Can we afford to lose email forwards? Do we want that?
Angela: I hate them. You send me these filthy emails and you say forward them to ten people or you'll have bad luck.
Michael: Give me a break. Umm... Stanley, how about that hot picture you have by your desk? Centerfold in the Catholic schoolgirl's outfit? I mean, it is hot, it is sexy, and it turns him on. And I will admit, best part of my morning is staring at it. But what? Are we just going to take it away?
Stanley: That is my daughter. She goes to Catholic girls' school. I am taking it down right now.
Meredith: Um... what about office romance?
Toby: Office relationships are never a good idea. Yeah. So let's just try to avoid them. But, um, if you already have one, you should disclose it to HR.
Phyllis: All relationships? Eh, even a one-night stand?
Michael: I think the old honor system was just fine. For example, I have never slept with an employee. And, believe me, I could have.
Dwight: Yeah, Meredith.
Michael: No! No! Catherine. Remember her? Remember how hot she was?
Dwight: Yes.
Michael: She would have definitely slept with me.
Kevin: She wasn't that hot.
Michael: Yes, she was. Dammit, Kevin!
Toby: Ok, you know, Michael...
scene_arc: The seminar ends with Pam asking for good behavior. Michael arrives and expands into a fifteen-minute rant about what they're losing — email forwards, Stanley's daughter photo, Catherine — before Toby shuts it down.
motivation: To name email forwards as the thing being lost, then immediately qualify that she hates them.
internal_thoughts: He asked what we're losing. Email forwards. I said it because it's accurate. Then I said I hate them. Both things are true.
reflection: I named the thing and then immediately said I hate it. That was internally consistent.

title: Kevin, Oscar, and Angela receive a forwarded email with an image of a Senator; Angela says it's fake because she's a Senator | observation
given_circumstances: Kevin, Oscar, and Angela are receiving a forwarded email with an image of a Senator.
full_dialogue:
Kevin: [Email chiming] [snickers] Did you see that?
Oscar: I saw it.
Kevin: You can see her...
Angela: It's fake.
Kevin: How do you know?
Angela: She wouldn't do that. She's a Senator.
scene_arc: Kevin, Oscar, and Angela receive a forwarded email with an image of a Senator. Angela says it's fake. Kevin asks how she knows. Angela says she's a Senator.
motivation: To call the image fake and explain why.
internal_thoughts: It's fake. Kevin asked how I know. She's a Senator. Senators don't do that.
reflection: She's a Senator. That's the full explanation.

title: Jim joins Hate Ball; Kevin names it; Oscar introduces Dunderball | joy
given_circumstances: Jim has joined the paper football game. Angela reacts. The group discovers additional games.
full_dialogue:
Jim: [flicks football onto Kevin's desk] OH!
Kevin: Oh!
Oscar: Sweet!
Jim: Yes! So close. I really love the paper triangle flicking and hitting things game. Yeah.
Kevin: We call it Hate Ball.
Jim: Why?
Kevin: Because of how much Angela hates it.
Jim: Hey, do you guys have any other games?
Kevin: Sometimes we play 'Who can put the most M&M's in their mouth?'
Angela: You
Oscar: You should ask Toby to teach you Dunderball.
scene_arc: Jim joins Hate Ball. Angela reacts. Kevin names it. Oscar introduces Dunderball.
motivation: To express blanket contempt for the paper football game.
internal_thoughts: You. That's all I said. I was going to say the word you use before 'are disgusting' or similar. I stopped at you.
reflection: I said you. That covered it.

title: Angela explains she plays games at home with her cats, just not at work | character
given_circumstances: Angela is speaking to the documentary camera about her relationship with games.
full_dialogue: Angela: I do play games. I sing and I dangle things in front of my cats. I play lots of games. Just not at work.
scene_arc: Angela explains she plays games at home with her cats, just not at work.
motivation: To clarify that she does play games, just not at work.

Angela: Do not panic.

title: Angela would take DaVinci Code only to burn it; Dwight hollows out a Physician's Desk Reference and packs survival supplies | character

Angela: No.

Angela: The DaVinci Code!

reflection: I would take DaVinci Code in order to burn it. Seven seconds of warmth. Still worth it.

title: The suggestion box meeting: Y2K, Tom the suicide, B.O., coffee breath, gum, and Dwight reading don't sleep with your boss out loud | consequence

I just sift through them every week and I really look and scrutinize to see what you guys are writing. Um, so, let's, uh, just read some of these suckers. Alright. Number one: 'What should we do to prepare for Y2K?'
Dwight: What should we do to prepare for Y2K?

Michael: Oh, that guy? That guy was weird. Alright, next suggestion.

Not that I would ever say something like that in public, and I never have, and I never will. I just think it's something that we should all be aware of. Ok? Now that we've learned this, let's continue. 'You need to do something about your coffee breath'-
Dwight: You need-
Michael: Ok.
Dwight: To do something about-
Michael: Shut up, shut up, shut up, Dwight, OK. I don't think you people are grasping the concept of the suggestion box.

Michael: Yeah, what? Uh, alright, next suggestion.
